quizQuestion: Who wrote what is considered the first published algorithm intended for execution by a machine — and her notes were three times longer than the original paper she was translating?
explanation: Ada Lovelace translated Luigi Menabrea's 1842 paper on Babbage's Analytical Engine and appended her own notes, which were three times longer than the original. Those notes included what is now recognised as the first published algorithm designed for machine execution.
visualDescription: A Victorian-era woman in formal dress seated at a large mechanical calculating engine, writing in a leather notebook by gaslight

quizQuestion: In what year was Ada Lovelace — the daughter of poet Lord Byron and pioneer of computing — born?
explanation: Ada Lovelace was born Augusta Ada Byron on 10 December 1815. She was the only legitimate child of the poet Lord Byron, though she never knew him; her mother insisted on mathematics education to prevent her from inheriting Byron's 'dangerous' poetic temperament.
visualDescription: A formal portrait of a young Victorian woman, quill in hand, with a large mechanical gear mechanism in the background

quizQuestion: Who designed a general-purpose mechanical computer in the 1830s that borrowed its programming mechanism from the Jacquard loom — and never got to see it built?
explanation: Charles Babbage designed the Analytical Engine starting around 1837. It incorporated an arithmetic logic unit, conditional branching, loops, and memory — all essential ideas of modern computers. It borrowed the punch-card input idea from the Jacquard loom but was never constructed in Babbage's lifetime.
visualDescription: Blueprints of an enormous brass gear-and-lever computing machine spread across a Victorian engineer's drafting table

quizQuestion: In what year did Alan Turing publish 'On Computable Numbers', introducing the theoretical computing machine that bears his name?
explanation: Alan Turing published 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem' in 1936, introducing the Turing machine as a mathematical model of computation. This paper is considered the theoretical foundation of all computer science.
visualDescription: A stylised tape machine with binary symbols scrolling past a read-write head, set in a dimly lit 1930s mathematics library

quizQuestion: Who proposed a test for machine intelligence in 1950 by asking whether a computer's conversation could be indistinguishable from a human's?
explanation: Alan Turing published 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' in 1950, proposing what became known as the Turing Test. He framed it as an 'imitation game': a human judge conversing via text with a machine and a human, trying to tell which was which.
visualDescription: A 1950s room with a teleprinter on a desk, a person typing to an unseen respondent behind a partition

quizQuestion: In what year did John von Neumann's 'First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC' describe the stored-program architecture now used in virtually all computers?
explanation: Von Neumann's 1945 report described a computer architecture where instructions and data are stored in the same memory, allowing programs to be treated and modified like data. This 'von Neumann architecture' became the basis for nearly all modern computers.
visualDescription: A schematic diagram of a CPU, memory bank, and I/O channels connected by data buses, sketched in 1940s engineering style

quizQuestion: Who founded information theory with a 1948 paper that introduced the 'bit' as the fundamental unit of information?
explanation: Claude Shannon's 1948 paper 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication', published in the Bell System Technical Journal, laid the foundations of information theory and introduced the bit (binary digit) as the fundamental unit of information. Shannon is called the 'father of information theory'.
visualDescription: A mathematician juggling binary digits, surrounded by signal-and-noise graphs and telephone relay diagrams in a mid-century Bell Labs office

quizQuestion: Which US Navy Rear Admiral popularised the term 'computer bug' after her team found a literal moth trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II in 1947?
explanation: Grace Hopper's team at Harvard found an actual moth stuck in relay 70 of the Mark II computer on 9 September 1947. The moth was taped into the log book with the note 'First actual case of bug being found.' Hopper popularised the term 'debugging', though she credited the prior use of 'bug' in engineering.
visualDescription: A logbook from the 1940s with a moth taped to the page and handwritten annotation reading 'First actual case of bug being found'

quizQuestion: Who developed FLOW-MATIC, the business-oriented language that directly influenced the design of COBOL?
explanation: Grace Hopper devised the theory of machine-independent programming languages and used it to create FLOW-MATIC, the first English-language data processing compiler. FLOW-MATIC directly influenced the 1959 design of COBOL.
visualDescription: A Navy officer in uniform writing COBOL-like English-language code on a blackboard in a 1950s computing lab

quizQuestion: Who built the world's first functional program-controlled Turing-complete computer — largely in his parents' living room in Berlin — in 1941?
explanation: Konrad Zuse's Z3 became operational in May 1941, making it the world's first functional program-controlled Turing-complete computer. Remarkably, Zuse built his early computers with private funding, largely in his parents' apartment in Berlin.
visualDescription: A large mechanical relay-based computer filling a room in a 1940s Berlin apartment, a young engineer in a suit inspecting the wiring

quizQuestion: What was the name of the first high-level programming language, developed by Konrad Zuse around 1945, though not published until 1972?
explanation: Konrad Zuse developed Plankalkül (Plan Calculus) around 1945 as the first high-level programming language. It was designed for the Z4 computer but remained unpublished until 1972 and was not implemented until the 1970s, long after languages like FORTRAN and COBOL.
visualDescription: A German-language programming manuscript from the 1940s showing mathematical notation and flow diagrams on yellowed paper

quizQuestion: Who is called the 'father of the analysis of algorithms' and wrote 'The Art of Computer Programming', also creating the TeX typesetting system?
explanation: Donald Knuth, professor emeritus at Stanford, won the 1974 ACM Turing Award and is known as the 'father of the analysis of algorithms'. His multi-volume 'Art of Computer Programming' covers fundamental algorithms, and he created TeX when he was dissatisfied with how his books were being typeset.
visualDescription: A shelf of thick technical volumes labeled 'The Art of Computer Programming', beside a mathematical manuscript typeset with extraordinary precision

quizQuestion: Who wrote the famous 1968 letter titled 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful' that helped end unstructured use of GOTO in programming?
explanation: Edsger Dijkstra's 1968 letter in Communications of the ACM argued that GOTO statements made programs difficult to verify and understand, helping launch the structured programming movement. He also invented Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm in 1956 and won the 1972 ACM Turing Award.
visualDescription: A 1960s computer terminal with punch-card output, a programmer scowling at spaghetti-code flow diagrams on the wall

quizQuestion: In what year did Edsger Dijkstra devise his famous algorithm for finding the shortest paths in a weighted graph?
explanation: Dijkstra designed his shortest-path algorithm in 1956 while thinking about how to demonstrate the capabilities of the ARMAC computer. He reportedly conceived it in about 20 minutes without pencil and paper. It was published in a paper in 1959.
visualDescription: A weighted graph diagram with highlighted shortest-path edges glowing in a 1950s computing laboratory setting

quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'software engineering' and led the MIT team whose code prevented an abort of the Apollo 11 lunar landing?
explanation: Margaret Hamilton directed the Software Engineering Division at MIT that built the Apollo Guidance Computer software. She coined 'software engineering' to give software development the same credibility as hardware engineering. Her software's priority-scheduling prevented a 1202 alarm from aborting the Apollo 11 Moon landing.
visualDescription: A woman in a 1969 NASA control room standing beside a stack of printed code as tall as she is, mission control screens glowing in the background

quizQuestion: Who created the C programming language at Bell Labs, also co-creating Unix with Ken Thompson?
explanation: Dennis Ritchie created C at Bell Labs between 1969 and 1973, and co-created Unix with Ken Thompson starting in 1969. C became the foundation for operating systems, embedded systems, and countless later languages. Ritchie and Thompson shared the 1983 ACM Turing Award.
visualDescription: Two programmers at adjacent terminals in a 1970s Bell Labs office, one writing C code, the other debugging a Unix shell

quizQuestion: Which Unix co-creator also co-designed the Go programming language at Google, announced in 2009?
explanation: Ken Thompson, who co-designed Unix at Bell Labs in 1969, later co-designed the Go programming language at Google in 2007 alongside Robert Griesemer and Rob Pike. Thompson also invented the B programming language, the direct predecessor to C. He shared the 1983 Turing Award with Dennis Ritchie.
visualDescription: Side-by-side terminals showing Unix and Go code side by side, spanning decades from 1969 to 2009

quizQuestion: Who co-designed the TCP/IP protocol suite with Robert Kahn in the 1970s, earning the title 'father of the Internet'?
explanation: Vint Cerf and Robert Kahn co-designed TCP/IP in the 1970s, creating the fundamental communication protocol of the Internet. Both received the 2004 ACM Turing Award for this work. Cerf went on to become a VP at Google, still advocating for the open Internet.
visualDescription: A 1970s ARPANET node diagram showing packet routing across interconnected computers, with 'TCP/IP' handwritten in the margin

quizQuestion: In what year did Tim Berners-Lee propose an information management system at CERN that would become the World Wide Web?
explanation: Tim Berners-Lee submitted his proposal 'Information Management: A Proposal' on 12 March 1989 while working at CERN. His supervisor famously wrote 'Vague but exciting' on the cover. The first successful HTTP communication between client and server followed in late 1990.
visualDescription: A CERN office in 1989, a researcher's desk covered with network diagrams, a handwritten note reading 'Vague but exciting' on a proposal document

quizQuestion: Who invented HTML, the URL, and HTTP — and in doing so created the World Wide Web while working at CERN?
explanation: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web, HTML (HyperText Markup Language), the URL (Uniform Resource Locator) system, and HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) between 1989 and 1991. He later founded the W3C to develop open web standards and famously never patented his invention.
visualDescription: A NeXT computer workstation with the world's first website displayed on screen: 'info.cern.ch' describing the World Wide Web project

quizQuestion: In what year did Linus Torvalds release the first version of the Linux kernel, famously announcing it as 'just a hobby, won't be big and professional'?
explanation: Linus Torvalds created the Linux kernel in 1991 and announced it on a Usenet newsgroup with the now-legendary line: 'I'm doing a (free) operating system (just a hobby, won't be big and professional like gnu)'. Linux is now the most widely used kernel in the world, powering Android, most web servers, and all top 500 supercomputers.
visualDescription: A Finnish university student at a 1991 computer terminal posting to Usenet, with a penguin figurine on the desk

quizQuestion: What distributed version-control system did Linus Torvalds create in 2005 to manage Linux kernel development after disagreeing with the existing tool's licensing?
explanation: Linus Torvalds created Git in 2005 after the Linux kernel project lost access to the proprietary BitKeeper version control system. Torvalds designed Git to be fast, support distributed development, and be simple in design. It is now the world's most widely used version-control system.
visualDescription: A branching commit graph diagram on a whiteboard in a developer's home office, the Linux penguin pinned nearby

quizQuestion: Before being renamed to capitalise on Java's popularity, what was JavaScript originally called when Brendan Eich created it at Netscape in 1995?
explanation: Brendan Eich created JavaScript in 1995 at Netscape, where it was originally called LiveScript. It was renamed JavaScript for the public release to capitalise on Java's popularity at the time — despite the two languages having fundamentally different designs and being unrelated.
visualDescription: A 1995 Netscape browser window with 'LiveScript' visible in a tooltip, a 'Powered by Java' badge nearby

quizQuestion: What was Java originally called when James Gosling first developed it at Sun Microsystems — named after a tree outside his office window?
explanation: Java was initially called 'Oak' after an oak tree outside James Gosling's office at Sun Microsystems. When they discovered 'Oak' was already trademarked, the team renamed it 'Green', then finally 'Java' (after Java coffee). The language was publicly released in 1995.
visualDescription: A Sun Microsystems office window overlooking an oak tree, a developer at a workstation with early Java bytecode on screen

quizQuestion: Who created C++ at Bell Labs, starting in 1979 with a project he initially called 'C with Classes'?
explanation: Bjarne Stroustrup began developing C++ at Bell Labs in 1979 as 'C with Classes', adding object-oriented features to Dennis Ritchie's C language. It was first released externally as C++ in 1985. C++ became one of the most widely used languages for systems programming, game development, and performance-critical software.
visualDescription: A Bell Labs whiteboard covered in class hierarchy diagrams, an 'Inheritance' arrow pointing from a C box to a C++ box

quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'artificial intelligence' as co-author of the 1956 Dartmouth Workshop proposal and later created the Lisp programming language?
explanation: John McCarthy co-authored the 1956 Dartmouth Conference proposal that coined the term 'artificial intelligence'. He also created the Lisp programming language at MIT in 1958, invented the concept of time-sharing, and pioneered garbage collection. He won the 1971 ACM Turing Award for contributions to AI.
visualDescription: A 1956 Dartmouth College summer workshop attendees around a chalkboard, the words 'Artificial Intelligence' freshly written

quizQuestion: Who called the invention of the null reference his 'billion-dollar mistake' — yet also invented Quicksort and won the 1980 ACM Turing Award?
explanation: Tony Hoare invented Quicksort in 1959 and developed Hoare logic for formally verifying program correctness, winning the 1980 ACM Turing Award. He also invented the null reference and famously called it his 'billion-dollar mistake', estimating the cost of null pointer errors over subsequent decades.
visualDescription: A whiteboard with QUICKSORT pseudocode above and a large red X through 'null', with a '$1,000,000,000' price tag

quizQuestion: Who invented the relational model for databases in a landmark 1970 IBM paper that became the theoretical basis for SQL and all relational databases?
explanation: Edgar F. Codd published 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks' in 1970 while at IBM, inventing the theoretical basis for relational databases. His work directly influenced the creation of SQL and relational systems like Oracle and IBM DB2. He won the 1981 ACM Turing Award.
visualDescription: A 1970 IBM research paper with rows-and-columns table diagrams, the word 'Relational' circled in red ink

quizQuestion: Who gave the 'Mother of All Demos' in 1968, publicly unveiling the computer mouse, hypertext, video conferencing, and collaborative real-time editing in a single 90-minute presentation?
explanation: Douglas Engelbart's 9 December 1968 demonstration at the Fall Joint Computer Conference in San Francisco is called the 'Mother of All Demos'. In one presentation he introduced the computer mouse, hypertext links, video conferencing, windowing, and real-time collaborative editing — all entirely new concepts to the audience.
visualDescription: A 1968 auditorium with a projected computer screen showing a cursor moving via a small wooden box — the first public demo of a computer mouse

quizQuestion: Which Hollywood film star also co-invented frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology in 1942 — the concept that underpins modern Wi-Fi, GPS, and Bluetooth?
explanation: Hedy Lamarr, a major Hollywood film star, co-invented a radio guidance system using frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology with composer George Antheil. Their 1942 patent (US 2,292,387) described the technique to prevent enemy jamming of torpedo guidance. The technology was not adopted until after the patent expired, but it forms the conceptual basis for Wi-Fi, GPS, and Bluetooth.
visualDescription: A glamorous 1940s portrait split with a radio frequency diagram and patent document, bridging Hollywood and wartime engineering

quizQuestion: Who developed the lambda calculus in the 1930s — the formal system for expressing computation that underpins all functional programming languages?
explanation: Alonzo Church developed the lambda calculus in the 1930s as a formal system for expressing computation using function abstraction and application. He also independently proved the Entscheidungsproblem unsolvable in 1936. The Church-Turing thesis (with his doctoral student Alan Turing) formalises the intuitive notion of computability.
visualDescription: A 1930s Princeton mathematics office with blackboard equations featuring the Greek letter lambda, early mathematical logic papers stacked nearby

quizQuestion: Who led the IBM team that created FORTRAN in 1957 — the first widely used high-level programming language — and also invented the Backus-Naur Form grammar notation?
explanation: John Backus led the IBM team that delivered FORTRAN (Formula Translation) compilers in April 1957, creating the first widely used high-level programming language. He also invented Backus-Naur Form (BNF), the standard notation for defining programming language grammars, and won the 1977 ACM Turing Award.
visualDescription: A 1957 IBM punch-card deck labelled 'FORTRAN' beside a compiler manual, with mathematical equations being translated into machine instructions

quizQuestion: Who became the first woman to win the ACM Turing Award in 2006, recognised for foundational contributions to optimising compilers?
explanation: Frances Allen won the 2006 ACM Turing Award for foundational contributions to the theory and practice of optimising compilers, including her work on program optimisation frameworks. She worked at IBM from 1957 to 2002 and was the first woman to become an IBM Fellow.
visualDescription: A woman in a 1970s IBM research lab surrounded by compiler output listings, a Turing Award trophy reflected in the window

quizQuestion: Who was the first woman to earn a PhD in computer science from an American university, later formulating a key OOP principle that bears her name?
explanation: Barbara Liskov earned her PhD from Stanford in 1968, becoming the first woman to receive a computer science doctorate from an American university. She later formulated the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP), a foundational principle of object-oriented design, and won the 2008 ACM Turing Award.
visualDescription: A 1968 Stanford graduation ceremony, a woman in academic robes holding a computer science dissertation, surrounded by male graduates

quizQuestion: Who designed the Pascal programming language, won the 1984 ACM Turing Award, and coined the sardonic law that 'software is getting slower more rapidly than hardware becomes faster'?
explanation: Niklaus Wirth designed Pascal (1970), Modula-2, and Oberon, winning the 1984 ACM Turing Award for developing a sequence of innovative programming languages. He formulated Wirth's Law — a counterpoint to Moore's Law — observing that software bloat outpaces hardware improvements.
visualDescription: A Swiss computer science professor at a chalkboard with 'Pascal' written on one side and 'Software bloat > Moore's Law' on the other

quizQuestion: Who is nicknamed the 'Mother of the Internet' for inventing the Spanning Tree Protocol, a fundamental algorithm that prevents network loops in Ethernet bridges?
explanation: Radia Perlman invented the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) while working at Digital Equipment Corporation. STP is essential to the operation of network bridges and Ethernet networks, preventing broadcast storms by creating a loop-free logical topology. Despite the nickname 'Mother of the Internet', Perlman has said the Internet is a collective achievement.
visualDescription: A network diagram of Ethernet bridges with a spanning tree highlighted in green, routing data around a broken loop

quizQuestion: Who co-authored the 1980 textbook 'Introduction to VLSI Systems' that revolutionised chip design education and helped a generation of engineers design integrated circuits?
explanation: Lynn Conway co-authored 'Introduction to VLSI Systems' with Carver Mead in 1980, which established the Mead-Conway VLSI design rules and transformed how integrated circuits were taught and designed. Earlier, while at IBM, she had invented superscalar dynamic instruction handling — a key technology for modern out-of-order CPUs — before IBM fired her when she disclosed her gender transition.
visualDescription: A 1980 engineering textbook open to VLSI chip layout diagrams, beside a modern CPU showing billions of transistors at nanometre scale

quizQuestion: In what year did Geoffrey Hinton — 'the Godfather of AI' — share the Nobel Prize in Physics for discoveries enabling machine learning with artificial neural networks?
explanation: Geoffrey Hinton and John Hopfield shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics for foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks. Hinton, known as 'the Godfather of AI', had left Google in 2023 to speak more freely about AI risks.
visualDescription: A Nobel Prize medal beside a diagram of a deep neural network, surrounded by headlines about AI breakthroughs from 2024

quizQuestion: Which Stanford PhD student co-invented the PageRank algorithm — named after him — that became the foundation of Google's search engine?
explanation: Larry Page co-invented the PageRank algorithm with Sergey Brin while both were PhD students at Stanford. PageRank ranked web pages by the number and quality of links pointing to them. Page and Brin co-founded Google, incorporated on 4 September 1998.
visualDescription: A Stanford University computer lab in 1998, a whiteboard showing a directed graph of web pages with scores, 'PageRank' written in blue marker

quizQuestion: Who created Python and served as its 'benevolent dictator for life' until stepping down in 2018?
explanation: Guido van Rossum created Python in the late 1980s, releasing version 0.9.0 in 1991. He was Python's 'benevolent dictator for life' (BDFL) — holding final decision authority over the language's direction — until he stepped down on 12 July 2018. Python became one of the most popular languages in the world.
visualDescription: A Dutch programmer surrounded by Python documentation and a 'BDFL' crown, stepping away from a throne made of Python manuals
